danger-scale-1.heading: Degré de danger 1 — Faible
danger-scale-1.text:
Situation avalancheuse favorable en général

Aucun signe d’alarme n’est détectable. Les avalanches ne peuvent être déclenchées que de manière isolée, surtout sur les pentes extrêmement raides.

Recommandations pour les personnes en dehors des zones sécurisées

Descendre les pentes extrêmement raides un par un et tenir compte du danger de chute.

danger-scale-2.heading: Degré de danger 2 — Limité
danger-scale-2.text:
Situation avalancheuse favorable en général

Des signes d’alarme peuvent survenir de manière isolée. Des avalanches peuvent être déclenchées surtout sur les pentes très raides aux expositions et altitudes indiquées sur avalanch.report. Il ne faut pas s’attendre à d'importantes avalanches spontanées.

Recommandations pour les personnes en dehors des zones sécurisées

Choisir l’itinéraire avec prudence, surtout sur les pentes aux expositions et altitudes indiquées sur avalanche.report. Descendre les pentes très raides un par un. Prudence particulière en cas de structure défavorable du manteau neigeux (situation de neige ancienne).

danger-scale-3.heading: Degré de danger 3 — Marqué
danger-scale-3.text:
Situation avalancheuse critique

Des « woumf » et des fissures sont typiques. Des avalanches peuvent être facilement déclenchées surtout sur les pentes raides aux expositions et altitudes indiquées sur avalanche.report. Des avalanches spontanées et des déclenchements à distance sont possibles.

Recommandations pour les personnes en dehors des zones sécurisées

La situation la plus critique pour les adeptes des sports d’hiver ! Il est nécessaire de choisir l'itinéraire optimal et de mettre en œuvre des mesures visant à réduire les risques. Éviter les pentes très raides aux expositions et altitudes indiquées sur avalanche.report. Les personnes peu expérimentées ont tout intérêt à rester sur les pistes et les itinéraires balisés.

danger-scale-4.heading: Degré de danger 4 — Fort
danger-scale-4.text:
Situation avalancheuse très critique

Des avalanches spontanées et souvent très grandes sont probables. Des avalanches peuvent être facilement déclenchées sur de nombreux pentes raides. Les déclenchements à distance sont typiques. Des « woumf » et des fissures sont fréquents.

Recommandations pour les personnes en dehors des zones sécurisées

Se limiter aux terrains modérément raides. Faire attention aux zones de dépôt de très grandes avalanches. Les personnes peu expérimentées doivent rester sur les pistes et itinéraires balisés.

danger-scale-5.heading: Degré de danger 5 — Très fort
danger-scale-5.text:
Situation avalancheuse exceptionnelle

De nombreux départs spontanés d'avalanches très grandes et d’ampleur exceptionnelle sont attendus. Elles peuvent atteindre des routes et des localités en fond de vallées.

Recommandations pour les personnes en dehors des zones sécurisées

Il est déconseillé de pratiquer des sports d'hiver en dehors des pistes de ski et des sentiers balisés.

avalanche-frequency-none.heading: Fréquence : Aucune ou très rares
avalanche-frequency-none.text: La fréquence indique le nombre prévu de sites exposés aux avalanches pour une classe de stabilité du manteau neigeux donnée dans la zone d'alerte. Pour la classe aucune ou très rares, les sites classés selon la classe de stabilité du manteau neigeux {stabilityClass} n'existent pas ou sont si rares qu'ils ne sont pas jugés pertinents pour l'évaluation de la stabilité.

avalanche-frequency-few.heading: Fréquence : Rares
avalanche-frequency-few.text: La fréquence indique le nombre prévu de sites exposés aux avalanches pour une classe de stabilité du manteau neigeux donnée dans la zone d'alerte. Pour la classe rares, les sites classés selon la classe de stabilité du manteau neigeux {stabilityClass} sont rares. Bien qu'ils soient rares, leur nombre est jugé pertinent pour l'évaluation de la stabilité.

avalanche-frequency-some.heading: Fréquence : Quelques
avalanche-frequency-some.text: La fréquence indique le nombre prévu de sites exposés aux avalanches pour une classe de stabilité du manteau neigeux donnée dans la zone d'alerte. Pour la classe quelques, les sites classés selon la classe de stabilité du manteau neigeux {stabilityClass} se trouvent généralement dans des formations topographiques spécifiques présentant des caractéristiques communes (par exemple, à proximité des crêtes, dans les combes).

avalanche-frequency-many.heading: Fréquence : Nombreuses
avalanche-frequency-many.text: La fréquence indique le nombre prévu de sites exposés aux avalanches pour une classe de stabilité du manteau neigeux donnée dans la zone d'alerte. Pour la classe nombreuses, les sites classés selon la classe de stabilité du manteau neigeux {stabilityClass} sont nombreux.

snowpack-stability-very_poor.heading: Stabilité du manteau neigeux : Très instable
snowpack-stability-very_poor.text:
La stabilité du manteau neigeux indique la facilité avec laquelle des avalanches peuvent se déclencher. Un manteau neigeuxtrès instable signifie que les avalanches se déclenchent spontanément ou sont très faciles à déclencher (sensibles). Les avalanches naturelles, les craquements et les « woumfs » sont des signes évidents d'un manteau neigeuxtrès instable.

La fréquence et la stabilité du manteau neigeux sont étroitement liées. La fréquence indique le nombre prévu de sites exposés aux avalanches pour une classe de stabilité du manteau neigeux donnée, ici très instable, dans la zone d'alerte.

snowpack-stability-poor.heading: Stabilité du manteau neigeux : Instable
snowpack-stability-poor.text:
The snowpack stability indicates you how easy it is to trigger an avalanche. Poor snowpack stability means that avalanches are easy to trigger by e.g. a single skier. Whumpf-sounds and shooting cracks are clear signs for a poor snowpack stability.

Frequency and snowpack stability are closely related. The frequency indicates the expected number of avalanche-prone locations for a specific snowpack stability class, here poor, within the warning region.

snowpack-stability-fair.heading: Snowpack stability: Fair
snowpack-stability-fair.text:
La stabilité du manteau neigeux indique la facilité avec laquelle des avalanches peuvent se déclencher. Stabilité moyenne signifie que les avalanches sont difficiles à déclencher. Large loads such as multiple skiers, explosives or cornice falls are typically required to trigger an avalanche with fair snowpack stability conditions.

Frequency and snowpack stability are closely related. The frequency indicates the expected number of avalanche-prone locations for a specific snowpack stability class, here fair, within the warning region.

snowpack-stability-good.heading: Snowpack stability: Good
snowpack-stability-good.text:
The snowpack stability indicates you how easy it is to trigger an avalanche. Good stability means stable conditions. There are no signs of instability under good snowpack stability conditions.

Frequency and snowpack stability are closely related. The frequency indicates the expected number of avalanche-prone locations for a specific snowpack stability class, here good, within the warning region.

avalanche-size-1.heading: Size 1: Small avalanche (sluff)
avalanche-size-1.text: With avalanches of this size, it is unlikely that persons are buried, except in run out zones with adverse terrain features (i.e. terrain traps). In extremely steep terrain, the danger of falling prevails the danger of burials. Avalanches typically stop before the end of a slope.

avalanche-size-2.heading: Size 2: Medium avalanche
avalanche-size-2.text: It is likely that avalanches could bury, injure or kill a person. Avalanches of this size correspond to the typical skier-triggered avalanche. They stop typically at the end of a slope.

avalanche-size-3.heading: Size 3: Large avalanche
avalanche-size-3.text: It is likely that avalanches could bury and destroy a car, damage a truck, destroy a small building or break a few trees. When skiers are caught by avalanches of this size, probability for severe consequences are very high. Avalanche could traverse flat terrain (considerably below 30°) over distances of up to 50 m.

avalanche-size-4.heading: Size 4: Very large avalanche
avalanche-size-4.text: It is likely that avalanches could bury and destroy a railway car, large truck, several buildings or a piece of forest. Avalanches will traverse flat terrain (considerably below 30°) over distances more than 50 m and can reach valley ground.

avalanche-size-5.heading: Size 5: Extremely large avalanche
avalanche-size-5.text: It is likely that avalanches could gouge the landscape and have disastrous damage potential. Avalanche will reach valley ground and might flow the largest runout distance known.

problem-new_snow.heading: Avalanche problem: New snow
problem-new_snow.text:
The avalanche problem is related to current or most recent snowfall. The amount of additional loading by new snow onto the existing snowpack is the crucial factor of the new snow problem. How critical the loading is depends on various factors such as temperature or characteristics of the old snow surface.

Travel advice
Dry-snow slab avalanches: Wait until the snowpack stabilizes.
Dry loose snow avalanches: Danger of falling is more important than danger of burial. Consider consequences in steep terrain.

problem-wind_slab.heading: Avalanche problem: Wind slab
problem-wind_slab.text:
The avalanche problem is related to wind slabs. Snow can be transported by wind with or without a concurrent snowfall.

Travel advice
Avoid snowdrift deposits in steep terrain, in particular in areas where the snow cover changes from thin to thick or from hard to soft.

problem-persistent_weak_layers.heading: Avalanche problem: Persistent weak layers
problem-persistent_weak_layers.text:
The avalanche problem is related to the presence of persistent weak layers in the old snowpack. These weak layers typically include buried surface hoar, depth hoar or faceted crystals.

Travel advice
Travel conservatively and avoid large steep slopes. Consider the history of weather and snow cover processes in the area. Be extra cautious in areas with a thin snowpack and at the transition from thin to deep snowpack. This problem is a major cause of recreational avalanche fatalities.

problem-wet_snow.heading: Avalanche problem: Wet snow
problem-wet_snow.text:
The avalanche problem is related to weakening of the snowpack due to the presence of liquid water. Water infiltrates the snowpack due to melt or rain.

Travel advice
In the presence of a sun crust the conditions after cold nights with clear skies are usually favourable in the morning due to freezing. After warm nights with overcast skies the problem often exists already in the morning. Normally rain on fresh snow creates this problem almost immediately. Good timing and trip planning are important. Consider avalanche runout zones.

problem-gliding_snow.heading: Avalanche problem: Gliding snow
problem-gliding_snow.text:
The entire snowpack is gliding on the ground, typically on smooth ground such as grassy slopes or smooth rock zones. High activity of glide-snow avalanches are typically related to a thick snowpack with no or only few weak layers. Glide snow avalanches can occur both with a cold dry snowpack and with a warm wet snowpack. The release of a glide-snow avalanche is difficult to predict, although glide cracks open usually before a release.

Travel advice
Avoid areas close to glide cracks.

danger-pattern-DP1.heading: dp.1 deep persistent weak layer
danger-pattern-DP1.text:
At the onset of winter, slab avalanches are often observed following the second heavy snowfall, usually at high altitudes (>2000 m) and high alpine altitudes (>3000 m) on shady, very steep slopes. This is because in autumn after the first big snowfalls, a lengthy period of stable, highpressure weather follows. That is when ideal conditions prevail for the partial metamorphism of snow into loose crystals, which then form a deep persistent weak layer.

Watch the video on YouTube

danger-pattern-DP2.heading: dp.2 gliding snow
danger-pattern-DP2.text:
Glide-snow avalanches are among the most difficult avalanches to predict, since they can trigger literally at any time of day or night, even when the snow situation is at its most stable. That includes the coldest day and the warmest day of the winter. Furthermore, glide-snow avalanches are not triggered by additional loading.

Watch the video on YouTube

danger-pattern-DP3.heading: dp.3 rain on snow
danger-pattern-DP3.text:
Rain-on-snow is considered the classic alarm signal in snow and avalanche science because, on the one hand, its additional weight burdens the entire snowpack; and on the other, because the firmness of the snowpack is immediately lost. For those reasons, avalanches are predestined. Rain can occur at any time during the winter. The big advantage is that no danger sign could be simpler to recognize.

Watch the video on YouTube

danger-pattern-DP4.heading: dp.4 cold on warm / warm on cold
danger-pattern-DP4.text:
For the most part, large temperature differences during snowfall have a negative effect, since they reinforce snow metamorphism inside the snowpack. As a rule, a thin, weak layer forms which is highly prone to triggering. This layer is often found on south-facing slopes. It is highly treacherous for the additional reason that the weak layer does not yet exist immediately after the new snow is deposited, it forms only over the course of the following days.

Watch the video on YouTube

danger-pattern-DP5.heading: dp.5 snowfall after long period of cold
danger-pattern-DP5.text:
Among avalanche events, this is a classic: following a lengthy period of cold, it begins to snow. The problem is that fresh snowdrifts are deposited on wind-protected leeward slopes on top of a loose old snow cover surface which usually contains depth hoar. Wind slab and persistent weak layers are very poorly bonded with each other. The snowpack is just waiting to be triggered by additional loading.

Watch the video on YouTube

danger-pattern-DP6.heading: dp.6 loose snow and wind
danger-pattern-DP6.text:
'Wind is the architect of avalanches.' This classic proverb from the 1930s by Wilhelm Paulcke remains unchanged even today. Wind influences both snow which is falling and snow which has already been deposited. It is a major factor in the formation of avalanches. When the snow is loose and dry, wind transports it, thereby increasing avalanche danger. The colder the transported snow, the more sensitively it reacts to additional loading, because it is so brittle.

Watch the video on YouTube

danger-pattern-DP7.heading: dp.7 shallow snow next to deep snow
danger-pattern-DP7.text:
Areas where the snow is shallow usually have less favourable snowpack layering than where the snow is deep. This is because of amplified transformation processes which go on inside a shallow snowpack. Furthermore, slab avalanches are easier to trigger where the snow is shallow because the weak layers are not as deeply embedded in the snowpack, thus are more liable to trigger through additional loading. For that reason, avalanche triggerings are often observed in transition zones from shallow snow to deep snow.

Watch the video on YouTube

danger-pattern-DP8.heading: dp.8 buried surface hoar
danger-pattern-DP8.text:
Surface hoar numbers among the most beautiful types of snow in existence. In and of itself, it bears no danger potential. That potential unfolds only when it is buried by fresh, bonded layers of snow. Then it becomes hazardous. In fact, it is considered one of the most critical weak layers in all of snow and avalanche science.

Watch the video on YouTube

danger-pattern-DP9.heading: dp.9 buried graupel
danger-pattern-DP9.text:
A weak layer inside a snowpack is often compared with ball bearings. This image is actually suitable only for graupel: a spherical, granular form of precipitation which is often deposited during showers such as thunderstorms during springtime. It is easy to grasp that snowdrifts accumulating atop of a layer of graupel can only be poorly bonded with it, so that the danger of avalanches increases. Graupel is frequently spread over very small surface areas. Without examining the snowpack itself it is difficult to spot, even for professionals. This is an extremely treacherous phenomenon. Fortunately, the problems it creates are short-lived.

Watch the video on YouTube

danger-pattern-DP10.heading: dp.10 springtime situation
danger-pattern-DP10.text:
Avalanche danger is never easier to evaluate than in stable corn-snow conditions; on the other, avalanches of such magnitude as in springtime are rarely registered in deep winter. Apart from the snowpack layering, the complex interaction of air temperature, air moisture, solar radiation and wind play a major role. For backcountry skiers and boarders, high discipline in timing and flexibility of route planning are more essential than ever.

Watch the video on YouTube
